steps.title: Pin down the claim
steps.content: The claim to illustrate: Whitman "deeply understands the reader, whom he addresses directly." The supporting quotation must show Whitman perceiving/knowing the reader.

steps.title: Test for direct perception of the reader
steps.content: "Your true soul and body appear before me" explicitly says the speaker perceives the reader's true self — precisely a claim of deep understanding.

steps.title: Per-choice elimination
steps.content:
• Choice A — "Your true soul and body appear before me.": speaker claims to see the reader's true self — deep understanding. ✓
  • Choice B — "Whoever you are, now I place my hand upon you, that you be my poem.": claims a relationship/possession, not insight into the reader. ✗
  • Choice C — "I should have made my way straight to you long ago.": expresses regret about timing, not understanding the reader. ✗
  • Choice D — "Whoever you are, I fear you are walking the walks of dreams.": expresses worry/uncertainty about the reader, the opposite of deep understanding. ✗
A is correct.
